approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² log M)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all pairs of elements, compute GCD for each pair using Euclidean algorithm, and sum results. This is the most straightforward implementation but impractical for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force GCD Convolution
ladders.desc: Compute sum of GCDs for all pairs in an array using O(n² log M) approach
ladders.explanation:
The GCD convolution problem requires computing the sum of GCDs for all possible pairs of elements in an array. The brute force approach is conceptually simple: iterate through every possible pair (i,j) where i ≤ j, compute their GCD using the Euclidean algorithm, and accumulate the results. The intuition is straightforward - we need to consider all interactions between elements, and the most direct way is to examine each pair individually. The algorithm starts by initializing a sum variable to 0. Then, for each element at index i from 0 to n-1, we consider every element at index j from i to n-1 (to avoid double counting). For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]), we compute their GCD using the Euclidean algorithm, which repeatedly applies the modulo operation until the remainder is 0. This GCD value is added to the running sum. The time complexity is O(n² log M) where n is the array length and M is the maximum element value - we have O(n²) pairs and each GCD computation takes O(log M) time. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This approach connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution. Common mistakes include: 1) Using i < j instead of i ≤ j (missing self-pairs), 2) Not handling zero values properly in GCD computation, 3) Using inefficient GCD implementations. The real code pattern involves nested loops with GCD computation in the inner loop.

Pseudocode:
function gcd_convolution_brute(arr):
    n = length(arr)
    total = 0
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        for j from i to n-1:
            a = arr[i]
            b = arr[j]
            # Compute GCD using Euclidean algorithm
            while b != 0:
                temp = b
                b = a % b
                a = temp
            gcd_val = a
            total += gcd_val
    return total

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Euclidean GCD Algorithm
ladders.desc: Compute greatest common divisor of two numbers using Euclidean algorithm
ladders.explanation:
The Euclidean algorithm is the fundamental subroutine for computing GCDs efficiently. The intuition is based on the mathematical property that GCD(a,b) = GCD(b, a mod b). This works because any common divisor of a and b must also divide their difference, and by extension, the remainder. The algorithm repeatedly replaces the larger number with the remainder of dividing the larger by the smaller until one number becomes 0, at which point the other number is the GCD. Step-by-step: 1) Take two numbers a and b. 2) While b is not 0: compute remainder r = a mod b, set a = b, set b = r. 3) When b becomes 0, a contains the GCD. The time complexity is O(log min(a,b)) because each step reduces the numbers significantly. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the parent problem by providing the GCD computation needed for each pair. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling zero values (GCD(a,0) = a), 2) Infinite loops if not updating variables correctly, 3) Using subtraction instead of modulo (less efficient). The real code pattern is a while loop with modulo operations.

Pseudocode:
function gcd(a, b):
    while b != 0:
        temp = b
        b = a % b
        a = temp
    return a

examples.trace:
Step 1: a=48, b=18, b≠0
   Compute r = 48 % 18 = 12
   Update: a = 18, b = 12
Step 2: a=18, b=12, b≠0
   Compute r = 18 % 12 = 6
   Update: a = 12, b = 6
Step 3: a=12, b=6, b≠0
   Compute r = 12 % 6 = 0
   Update: a = 6, b = 0
Step 4: b=0, so GCD = a = 6

Step 1: a=17, b=13, b≠0
   Compute r = 17 % 13 = 4
   Update: a = 13, b = 4
Step 2: a=13, b=4, b≠0
   Compute r = 13 % 4 = 1
   Update: a = 4, b = 1
Step 3: a=4, b=1, b≠0
   Compute r = 4 % 1 = 0
   Update: a = 1, b = 0
Step 4: b=0, so GCD = a = 1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise GCD Summation
ladders.desc: Iterate through all pairs of elements and sum their GCDs

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 6]
n=2
Initialize total=0
i=0 (value=3):
  j=0: GCD(3,3)=3, total=3
  j=1: GCD(3,6)=3, total=6
i=1 (value=6):
  j=1: GCD(6,6)=6, total=12
Return total=12

ladders.title: Building Block — Nested Loop Iteration Over Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all index pairs (i,j) with i≤j using nested loops

examples.trace:
n=2
Initialize empty list
i=0:
  j=0: add (0,0)
  j=1: add (0,1)
i=1:
  j=1: add (1,1)
Final list: [(0,0), (0,1), (1,1)]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Computation
ladders.desc: Compute remainder of division between two integers
ladders.explanation:
The modulo operation is fundamental to the Euclidean algorithm. It computes the remainder when one integer is divided by another. Formally, a mod b = r where a = b*q + r with 0 ≤ r < b. The intuition is that it tells us how much is left over after dividing a by b as evenly as possible. In programming, this is typically implemented with the % operator. For the Euclidean algorithm, we repeatedly compute a mod b to reduce the problem size. This connects to the GCD computation as the core arithmetic operation. Common mistakes include: 1) Confusing modulo with integer division, 2) Not handling negative numbers correctly (in most programming languages, a % b has the same sign as a), 3) Division by zero. The real code pattern is simply using the % operator.

Pseudocode:
function modulo(a, b):
    return a % b

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Greatest Common Divisor (GCD)
ladders.desc: Understand what GCD is and find it for small numbers by listing factors
ladders.explanation:
The greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers is the largest positive integer that divides both numbers without leaving a remainder. This is a foundational concept for the entire problem. The intuition is simple: we're looking for the biggest number that can evenly divide both given numbers. For example, GCD(12,18)=6 because 6 divides both 12 and 18, and no larger number does. To compute GCD manually for small numbers, we can list all factors of each number and find the largest common one. Step-by-step: 1) List all positive factors of the first number. 2) List all positive factors of the second number. 3) Find the common factors in both lists. 4) The largest common factor is the GCD. This connects to the modulo operation as it's the conceptual basis for why the Euclidean algorithm works. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting that GCD is always positive, 2) Missing factors, 3) Confusing GCD with LCM (least common multiple). The real code pattern for small numbers is factor listing, though inefficient for large numbers.

Pseudocode:
function gcd_naive(a, b):
    factors_a = all factors of a
    factors_b = all factors of b
    common = intersection(factors_a, factors_b)
    return max(common)

examples.trace:
Factors of 12: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12
Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18
Common factors: 1, 2, 3, 6
Largest common factor: 6
So GCD(12,18)=6

Factors of 7: 1, 7
Factors of 5: 1, 5
Common factors: 1
Largest common factor: 1
So GCD(7,5)=1

approaches.name: Dirichlet Convolution O(M log log M)
approaches.summary: Treat the problem as Dirichlet convolution of arithmetic functions. Use fast zeta and Möbius transforms on divisor lattices to compute the result in near-linear time relative to maximum value M.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Fast GCD Convolution via Dirichlet Convolution
ladders.desc: Compute the GCD convolution of two sequences using Dirichlet convolution with fast zeta and Möbius transforms.
ladders.explanation: The GCD convolution problem requires computing for each k from 1 to M: result[k] = sum_{gcd(i,j)=k} A[i] * B[j]. This can be transformed using Dirichlet convolution on arithmetic functions. Define f(d) = sum_{i: d|i} A[i] and g(d) = sum_{j: d|j} B[j]. Then h(d) = f(d) * g(d) gives sum_{gcd(i,j) is multiple of d} A[i]B[j]. Applying Möbius inversion: result[k] = sum_{d: k|d} μ(d/k) * h(d). The key is computing f and g via divisor zeta transform (O(M log log M)) and h via pointwise multiplication, then applying Möbius transform for result. Intuition: Instead of checking all pairs (i,j), aggregate contributions by their common divisors. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize arrays f, g, h, result of size M+1. 2. Compute f[d] = sum_{multiples m of d} A[m] using fast zeta transform: iterate primes p ≤ M, for d from M down to 1, if p*d ≤ M, add f[p*d] to f[d]. 3. Similarly compute g from B. 4. Compute h[d] = f[d] * g[d]. 5. Apply Möbius transform to h to get result: for each prime p, for d from 1 to M/p, result[d] -= result[p*d]; then result[k] = sum_{multiples m of k} μ(m/k) * h[m]. This works because Möbius inversion inverts the zeta transform. Time: O(M log log M) due to iterating over primes and multiples. Space: O(M). Connection: This integrates all sub-routines into the complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle μ(1)=1, off-by-one in array sizes, or incorrect loop directions. Real code pattern: def gcd_convolution(A, B, M): f = A[:]; g = B[:]; for p in primes(M): for d in range(M//p, 0, -1): f[d] += f[p*d]; g[d] += g[p*d]; h = [f[i]*g[i] for i in range(M+1)]; result = h[:]; for p in primes(M): for d in range(1, M//p+1): result[d] -= result[p*d]; return result[1:]

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize f = [0,1,2,3], g = [0,1,1,1]. Step 2: Zeta transform for primes up to 3 (primes: 2,3). For p=2: d from 1 to 1 (since M//2=1): f[1] += f[2] → f=[0,3,2,3]; g[1] += g[2] → g=[0,2,1,1]. For p=3: d from 1 to 1: f[1] += f[3] → f=[0,6,2,3]; g[1] += g[3] → g=[0,3,1,1]. Step 3: h = [0,18,2,3]. Step 4: Möbius transform: result = h copy = [0,18,2,3]. For p=2: d from 1 to 1: result[1] -= result[2] → result=[0,16,2,3]. For p=3: d from 1 to 1: result[1] -= result[3] → result=[0,13,2,3]. Step 5: result[1:] = [13,2,3] but need to adjust: Actually, after Möbius, result[k] = sum_{d multiple of k} μ(d/k) * h[d]. For k=1: μ(1)*h[1] + μ(2)*h[2] + μ(3)*h[3] = 1*18 + (-1)*2 + (-1)*3 = 13. For k=2: μ(1)*h[2] = 1*2 = 2. For k=3: μ(1)*h[3] = 1*3 = 3. So output [13,2,3]. Wait, check: Original problem: result[1] = A[1]B[1] + A[1]B[2] + A[1]B[3] + A[2]B[1] + A[3]B[1] + A[2]B[3] + A[3]B[2] = 1*1 + 1*1 + 1*1 + 2*1 + 3*1 + 2*1 + 3*1 = 1+1+1+2+3+2+3=13. Correct.

Step 1: f = [0,0,1,0], g = [0,0,0,1]. Step 2: Zeta: primes 2,3. p=2: d=1: f[1] += f[2] → f=[0,1,1,0]; g[1] += g[2] → g=[0,0,0,1]. p=3: d=1: f[1] += f[3] → f=[0,1,1,0]; g[1] += g[3] → g=[0,1,0,1]. Step 3: h = [0,1,0,0]. Step 4: Möbius: result = [0,1,0,0]. p=2: d=1: result[1] -= result[2] → [0,1,0,0]. p=3: d=1: result[1] -= result[3] → [0,1,0,0]. Then result[1]=1, but compute properly: For k=1: μ(1)*h[1]=1. For k=2: μ(1)*h[2]=0. For k=3: μ(1)*h[3]=0. So output [1,0,0]. Check original: Only pair with gcd=1 is (2,3) since A[2]=1, B[3]=1, gcd(2,3)=1, so result[1]=1. Others 0. Correct.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Fast Zeta Transform on Divisor Lattice
ladders.desc: Compute the sum over multiples for each divisor using prime iteration.
ladders.explanation: The zeta transform computes for each d from 1 to M: f[d] = sum_{m: d|m} A[m]. This aggregates values from multiples of d. Intuition: Instead of iterating all multiples for each d (O(M log M)), use a sieve-like approach by processing primes. For each prime p, update f[d] by adding f[p*d] for d from M/p down to 1. This works because any multiple m of d can be factored as d times a product of primes; processing primes sequentially accumulates contributions from all multiples. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize f as copy of A. 2. Generate list of primes up to M using sieve. 3. For each prime p in primes: for d from floor(M/p) down to 1: f[d] += f[p*d]. This ensures that when processing p, f[p*d] already includes contributions from multiples larger than p*d, so adding it to f[d] correctly accumulates. Time: O(M log log M) since each prime p iterates over M/p values, and sum of 1/p over primes is log log M. Space: O(M). Connection: This computes f and g in the full problem, which are then multiplied to get h. Common mistakes: Iterating d in increasing order (causes double counting), or missing primes. Real code pattern: def zeta_transform(arr, M): f = arr[:]; primes = sieve(M); for p in primes: for d in range(M//p, 0, -1): f[d] += f[p*d]; return f

ladders.title: Core Logic — Möbius Inversion for GCD Convolution
ladders.desc: Transform sum over multiples to sum over exact GCD using Möbius function.
ladders.explanation: Given h[d] = sum_{gcd(i,j) is multiple of d} A[i]B[j], we want result[k] = sum_{gcd(i,j)=k} A[i]B[j]. Möbius inversion relates these: result[k] = sum_{m: k|m} μ(m/k) * h[m]. Intuition: h counts pairs where gcd is a multiple of d (i.e., d divides gcd), which overcounts pairs with gcd exactly k. The Möbius function μ(n) is 0 if n has squared prime factor, 1 if n has even number of prime factors, -1 if odd. It acts as an inclusion-exclusion principle to subtract overcounts. Step-by-step: 1. Compute result array initially as copy of h. 2. For each prime p ≤ M: for d from 1 to M//p: result[d] -= result[p*d]. This applies Möbius transform because μ(p) = -1 for prime p, and composition for products. 3. Then result[k] gives the desired sum. Alternatively, directly compute: result[k] = sum_{m multiple of k} μ(m/k) * h[m]. Time: O(M log log M) similar to zeta. Space: O(M). Connection: This inverts the zeta transform to get the final convolution result. Common mistakes: Confusing direction of loops, or not initializing result correctly. Real code pattern: def mobius_transform(h, M): result = h[:]; primes = sieve(M); for p in primes: for d in range(1, M//p + 1): result[d] -= result[p*d]; return result

examples.trace: Step 1: result = [0,6,2,3]. Primes: 2,3. Step 2: p=2: d from 1 to 1: result[1] -= result[2] → result=[0,4,2,3]. Step 3: p=3: d=1: result[1] -= result[3] → result=[0,1,2,3]. But wait, this gives [0,1,2,3], but expected after inversion? Actually, need to compute result[k] = sum_{m multiple of k} μ(m/k) * h[m]. For k=1: μ(1)*h[1] + μ(2)*h[2] + μ(3)*h[3] = 1*6 + (-1)*2 + (-1)*3 = 1. So result[1]=1, which matches. So output [0,1,2,3]. Check: In full problem, after Möbius, result[1] becomes 1? But in earlier example, we had 13. Let's clarify: In full problem, h is after pointwise multiplication of f and g. Here h is given as example. So for this h, output is [0,1,2,3]. Correct.

Step 1: result = [0,1,0,0]. Primes: 2,3. p=2: d=1: result[1] -= result[2] → [0,1,0,0]. p=3: d=1: result[1] -= result[3] → [0,1,0,0]. So output [0,1,0,0]. Check: For k=1: μ(1)*h[1]=1. Others 0. Correct.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dirichlet Convolution of Arithmetic Functions
ladders.desc: Multiply two arithmetic functions via divisor sums.
ladders.explanation: Dirichlet convolution of two arithmetic functions f and g is defined as (f * g)(n) = sum_{d|n} f(d) g(n/d). For GCD convolution, we use a variant: given f(d) = sum_{i: d|i} A[i] and g(d) = sum_{j: d|j} B[j], then h(d) = f(d) * g(d) gives sum_{gcd(i,j) multiple of d} A[i]B[j]. Intuition: f(d) aggregates A values with indices divisible by d, similarly for g. Their product h(d) counts pairs (i,j) where both i and j are multiples of d, i.e., d divides both i and j, so d divides gcd(i,j). Thus h(d) overcounts pairs with gcd exactly k for k multiple of d. Step-by-step: 1. Compute f and g via zeta transform. 2. Compute h[d] = f[d] * g[d] for d=1..M. This is pointwise multiplication, O(M). Time: O(M) for multiplication, plus O(M log log M) for zeta. Space: O(M). Connection: This h is used in Möbius inversion to get exact GCD counts. Common mistakes: Not aligning indices (d from 1), or misunderstanding that h is not the final result. Real code pattern: def dirichlet_convolution(A, B, M): f = zeta_transform(A, M); g = zeta_transform(B, M); h = [0]*(M+1); for d in range(1, M+1): h[d] = f[d] * g[d]; return h

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointwise Array Multiplication
ladders.desc: Multiply corresponding elements of two arrays.
ladders.explanation: Given two arrays f and g of length N, compute h[i] = f[i] * g[i] for each i. This is a fundamental operation used in Dirichlet convolution. Intuition: It combines contributions from two sequences element-wise. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize h as empty array of size N. 2. For i from 0 to N-1: h[i] = f[i] * g[i]. Time: O(N). Space: O(N). Connection: In the full problem, this computes h from f and g after zeta transforms. Common mistakes: Index mismatches, or not handling zero-based indexing properly. Real code pattern: def pointwise_multiply(f, g): return [f[i] * g[i] for i in range(len(f))]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Summation Over Multiples
ladders.desc: Compute for each divisor d, the sum of values at indices that are multiples of d.
ladders.explanation: Given an array A indexed from 1 to M, compute f[d] = sum_{m: d divides m} A[m] for d=1..M. This is a basic aggregation problem. Intuition: For each d, we look at all indices m that are multiples of d (i.e., m = d, 2d, 3d, ... up to M) and sum their A values. Step-by-step naive algorithm: 1. Initialize f array of zeros size M+1. 2. For d from 1 to M: for m from d to M step d: f[d] += A[m]. Time: O(M log M) because sum of 1/d is log M. Space: O(M). Connection: This is the slow version of zeta transform; the fast version optimizes it using primes. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in loops, or not initializing f properly. Real code pattern: def sum_over_multiples(A, M): f = [0]*(M+1); for d in range(1, M+1): for m in range(d, M+1, d): f[d] += A[m]; return f
